Q1: I tried to keep a consistent routine, usually studying in the evenings after I finished any part-time work or chores at home. With about 27 hours a week dedicated to studying, I'd break it into chunks across the week, focusing on reading and writing notes since that's how I learn best. I kept a simple planner to track what needed to be done, though sometimes things got shifted around. It wasn't perfect, but I made it work with what I had.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. I tried to study at regular times, but when deadlines approached, everything intensified and I'd put in longer sessions. Some weeks were calmer and I could spread things out evenly, but other weeks I was scrambling. Life at home sometimes threw off my schedule too, so I had to be flexible and resilient about it.
Q3: Usually I logged into the LMS when I needed to check deadlines or download materials for my assignments. Sometimes I'd get a notification about something new being posted and that would prompt me to check. I also logged in to see if there were any announcements or if my peers had responded to discussion posts. It was more functional than habitual for me.
Q4: Honestly, the LMS never fully became a natural part of my routine. I used it when I needed to, but I wouldn't say I was logging in just to browse. Toward the middle of the semester, I got into a rhythm of checking it every couple of days, especially after connecting with some classmates through peer support. That connection made me want to stay more informed about what was happening in the course.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were the most useful for me because I learn best through reading and writing. I'd download PDFs and study guides and work through them carefully with my own notes. The discussion posts were also helpful when my peers shared their perspectives—it gave me different angles on the material. Videos were okay but I found myself preferring text-based content.
Q6: I often skipped the longer video lectures or only watched parts of them. With limited resources at home, streaming long videos wasn't always practical, and I found I retained more from reading anyway. Some of the optional supplementary materials I also delayed because I had to prioritize what was required first. Time was precious, so I had to be strategic.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to assignments or exams, or if it helped clarify something I was confused about. If I could see how it would improve my understanding or my grade, I was motivated to do it. Peer discussions also felt worthwhile because they helped me feel less alone in the struggle and sometimes surprised me with insights I hadn't considered.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't quite afford. Required ones had clear consequences if I didn't do them, so they got priority. With optional stuff, I'd think about doing it but then something more urgent would come up. It's not that I didn't value them—I just had to triage based on what would impact my grade the most.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download readings and work through them on paper, writing notes by hand because it helps me remember better. I also used some external resources like free online articles and study guides when the course materials weren't enough. My LMS activity probably doesn't capture half of the actual studying I did.
Q10: There were many evenings where I'd be at my desk with printed notes and a textbook, working through problems or re-reading concepts, and none of that showed up anywhere online. I'd also meet up with a classmate from the peer support group and we'd study together in person. Those sessions were really valuable but completely invisible to the system.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my main approach. I'd access materials once, download everything I needed, and then work through them offline over several days. It saved me from having to stay connected constantly and let me study during times when my internet wasn't reliable. So one download might represent hours of actual engagement.
Q12: There was a period mid-semester when I felt really anxious about whether I was keeping up. My assignment completion wasn't where I wanted it to be, and I started doubting myself. Competing responsibilities at home also piled up around that time. The peer support intervention actually helped me push through—having someone check in made a real difference when things got tough.
Q13: Definitely. When I was tired or overwhelmed with other responsibilities, I'd log into the LMS but not really engage deeply. I might skim something or just check what was due without actually absorbing the content. Confusion also made me hesitant sometimes—if I didn't understand something, I'd avoid it temporarily until I could figure out how to approach it.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd open a reading or start a video and realize my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd be thinking about things I needed to handle at home or feeling anxious about upcoming deadlines. Technically I was accessing materials, but I wasn't really present. I'd have to go back later and actually engage with it properly.
Q15: I postponed some assignments even when I knew they were important, usually because I felt overwhelmed or unsure how to start. The anxiety would build up and make it harder to just begin. Sometimes I also avoided discussion posts because I wasn't confident in my ideas. I've learned that starting is the hardest part, and once I push through that initial resistance, I can usually manage.
Q16: Deadlines basically dictated my LMS usage. I'd become much more active in the days leading up to a due date, downloading materials, checking requirements, and sometimes posting in discussions at the last minute. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have logged in as often. They were stressful but also necessary to keep me on track.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement. When I got comments on my work, whether positive or constructive, it motivated me to put more effort into the next assignment. Knowing someone was actually looking at my work made it feel more meaningful. The peer support also provided informal feedback that kept me going when official feedback was slow.
Q18: The course structure helped in some ways because it gave me clear milestones to work toward. But sometimes the pacing felt fast, and I'd fall behind on readings while trying to complete assignments. Having the materials organized in the LMS made it easier to find what I needed, though I wished there were more check-in points to help me gauge my progress.
Q19: The written resources being available for download made engagement much easier for me given my learning style and internet situation. What made it harder was when important information was only in video format or when deadlines clustered together. A more spread-out assignment schedule would have helped me manage my workload better without the last-minute stress.
Q20: I think sometimes students are genuinely trying to engage but struggling to translate that into completed work. They might be reading, researching, or even drafting things without submitting. It could also be anxiety or perfectionism—wanting to do well but feeling paralyzed about actually turning something in. High activity doesn't always mean high output, especially when life gets complicated.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like my experience. I did a significant amount of studying offline, so my LMS activity was lower than my actual engagement with the material. I passed the course and learned quite a bit, but you wouldn't necessarily see that in my login frequency. Some of us just work differently, and the system doesn't capture all the ways we learn.
Q22: I think course logs can accurately show when someone accesses materials and whether they submit work on time. But they miss the depth of engagement—whether someone actually understood what they read, how much time they spent thinking about it offline, or the conversations they had with peers outside the platform. They show behavior but not necessarily learning.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm actively thinking, questioning, and connecting ideas rather than just trying to get through a checklist. For me, it also involves feeling connected to the course community, even if that's just a few peers I can discuss things with. It's mental and emotional investment.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I relied on peer support to stay motivated, or how I overcame moments of anxiety to keep going. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with printed notes at my kitchen table, or the resilience it took to balance this course with limited resources. My engagement was real and consistent, just not always visible in the data.
